Notice Of And Motion For Reconsideration Of The Court'S Order On Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories Set No. 1, Award Of Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; And Declarations In Support Thereof
Off Calendar May-13-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT HIGH GROUND CONSTRUCTION, INC.'s Motion For Reconsideration Of The Court's Order On Plaintiff's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories Set No. 1, Award Of Sanctions.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion is untimely. Plaintiff served notice of entry of the court's order on March 28, 2025, by electronic service. The deadline for a reconsideration motion was 10 days plus two court days for electronic service, which the court calculates as April 9, 2025.

The court nonetheless exercises its inherent power to reconsider its prior discovery order in part. Many of the facts that Defendant relies on in seeking reconsideration were put before the court in opposition to Plaintiff's motion to compel the depositions of Jack Schnell and Defendant's PMQ. Those facts provide a basis for partial sua sponte reconsideration. The court modifies its prior order to permit Defendant's counsel to sign the verification. (See Melendrez v. Superior Court (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 1343, 1351.) (It's not clear to the court that this is even a modification: responses were ordered to be verified but the order did not specify who was to verify them.) The court further extends the time for Defendant to pay the ordered sanctions to July 21, 2025. The court does not reconsider the granting of sanctions. High Ground's ability to satisfy its discovery obligations became much more difficult after Mr. Schnell became incapacitated but the interrogatories at issue were originally propounded long before those events and should have been answered in full.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of court's ruling, with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of And Motion For Reconsideration Of The Court'S Order On Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Compliance With Plaintiff'S Requests For Production, Set One; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; And Declarations In Support Thereof
Off Calendar May-13-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT HIGH GROUND CONSTRUCTION, INC.'s Motion For Reconsideration Of The Court's Order On Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Compliance With Plaintiff's Requests For Production, Set One.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion is untimely. Plaintiff served notice of entry of the court's order on March 28, 2025, by electronic service. The deadline for a reconsideration motion was 10 days plus two court days for electronic service, which the court calculates as April 9, 2025. The court declines to exercise its inherent power to reconsider. The prior order said that responses must be verified but did not specify whom. Melendrez v. Superior Court (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 1343, recognizes that an attorney may verify discovery responses for an entity party. (Id. at p. 1346 ["the law provides that an attorney can verify responses on behalf of a corporation"].) No leave of court is required to exercise this option, but only a willingness to suffer the partial waiver of attorney-client and work product protections that such verification entails.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of court's ruling, with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Off Calendar May-01-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF YAN GROSSMAN's Motion To Compel Depositions Of High Ground Construction, Inc.'s Person(S) Most Knowledgeable And Jack Schnell, And Accompanying Document Production, Request For Monetary, Issue And Evidentiary Sanctions.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Yan Grossman's motion to compel depositions of High Ground Construction, Inc.'s person most qualified and of Jack Schnell, and for production of documents, and for monetary and evidentiary sanctions, is granted in part and denied in part.

High Ground is a dissolved corporation. Schnell was its principal and is apparently the only individual who could give testimony on its behalf. Schnell suffered a major medical event while the case was pending and now suffers from dementia. (Zunker Dec. paras. 6, 9.) Respecting Schnell, the court rejects the argument that he was improperly served through High Ground's counsel; he is the only officer of High Ground. But the court accepts that he cannot be deposed and is not competent to testify. The court orders the conditional evidentiary sanction that he may not offer testimony at trial unless he recovers and submits to deposition by the discovery cutoff in this case.

Respecting High Ground, the court orders the conditional evidentiary sanction that it may not offer testimony at trial on any matter covered in the list of PMQ topics attached as Exhibit I to the Berestka Declaration unless it identifies another deponent as PMQ who is qualified to provide testimony about the listed topics within 10 days of entry of this order and makes that PMQ available for deposition on a mutually agreed date within 25 days.

Grossman also seeks an order for production of documents listed in the deposition notices attached as Exhibits H and I to the Berestka Declaration. The court denies relief; no separate statement was provided and the moving papers do not set forth specific facts showing good cause for production. (See Rule of Court 3.1345(a)(5); CCP 2025.450(b)(1).)

Grossman also seeks monetary sanctions. In light of the evidentiary sanctions the court has ordered, and the circumstances that caused Schnell and High Ground to be unable to provide testimony, the court finds that imposition of monetary sanctions would be unjust and does not award them.

Order submitted.

Hearing is held. ARGUED The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF YAN GROSSMAN'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories Request For Sanctions.

Plaintiff's motion to compel further responses to Form Interrogatories, Set One - Construction, Nos. 304.1 and 304.3 is granted. The motion was unopposed. The requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) Plaintiff has shown that defendant High Ground Construction, Inc.'s responses are incomplete, and defendant has failed to demonstrate that any objection has merit. The court orders defendant to supplement its responses to 304.1 and 304.3 with a complete and straightforward answer within 10 court days of entry of this order. Responses shall be verified. Sanctions are mandatory unless the court finds that the party resisting discovery acted with substantial justification or other circumstances make imposition of a sanction unjust. (CCP Section 2030.290(c).) The court does not find substantial justification and orders Defendant to pay $3,041.50 in sanctions to Plaintiff within 10 court days of entry of this order.

Proposed order is submitted.
